skill.name: frontend-code-review
skill.description: Reviews frontend TypeScript and JavaScript changes against Dify-specific quality, performance, and business logic rules.
skill.summary: Frontend code review checklist for React, Tailwind CSS, React Flow, and Dify workflow components.
skill.author: langgenius

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network indicators. Review found false positives from Markdown backticks, file extensions, examples, and one GitHub issue link. No executable scripts, shell execution, credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: External Command Matches Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, file extensions, and review templates. They do not execute commands or instruct the model to run untrusted shell input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown text or code examples. I found no shell interpreter, script file, or instruction that executes these snippets.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic APIs or hash functions. The cited lines contain headings, descriptions, JSX snippets, and template placeholders used for frontend review guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain Markdown documentation, not implementation code. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak cryptographic operation appears in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Public Issue Reference
low_findings.description: The only network finding is a GitHub issue URL used as documentation for a frontend business logic rule. The skill does not fetch the URL or send data to it.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears inside explanatory Markdown text. There is no network API call, executable script, or data-bearing request associated with it.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Match Is Frontend Guidance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alert points to React Flow hook guidance. It is about component state access and does not collect host, user, process, or network environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line discusses useNodes, useEdges, and useStoreApi in React Flow. No system discovery command or host information access is present.

content.user_title: Review Frontend Code Against Dify Rules
content.value_statement: Frontend reviews can miss project-specific React, Tailwind CSS, and workflow rules. This skill applies a focused checklist and returns prioritized findings with suggested fixes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews changed frontend files or specific files named by the user.
Checks conditional class names against shared utility function usage.
Flags new CSS module usage when Tailwind CSS can handle the styling.
Checks React Flow node and edge access patterns for performance issues.
Identifies complex props that should use memoization before reaching child components.
Warns when node components depend on workflowStore where a provider may be absent.
limitations: It only covers the rules listed in its Markdown reference files.
It does not run tests, build commands, or static analysis by itself.
It is focused on frontend TypeScript and JavaScript files.
It may need repository context to confirm whether a pattern is truly reachable.

use_cases.title: Pre-Commit Frontend Review
use_cases.description: Review staged or changed React files before opening a pull request.

use_cases.title: Dify Workflow Component Audit
use_cases.description: Check node components for workflowStore usage that can break template-based RAG Pipe creation.

use_cases.title: Reviewer Checklist Support
use_cases.description: Apply a consistent checklist for Tailwind CSS, class names, React Flow usage, and memoized props.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of pending frontend changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my pending frontend changes with the frontend-code-review checklist. Report urgent issues first, then suggestions.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Component
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of a named file
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component with the frontend-code-review rules: path/to/component.tsx. Focus on class names, Tailwind CSS, memoization, and React Flow usage.

prompt_templates.title: Review Workflow Nodes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the workflow node components I changed. Check for workflowStore dependencies and any React Flow hook issues that can cause blank screens.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Prioritize Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before a pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these frontend changes and prioritize findings by urgent defects and improvement suggestions. Include file paths, line numbers, and concise fixes.

output_examples.input: Review a changed React component that builds class names with template strings.
output_examples.output: The review flags an urgent code quality issue, explains that shared classNames or cn should be used, and suggests a concise replacement.

output_examples.input: Review a workflow node component that imports a workflowStore hook.
output_examples.output: The review reports a business logic issue, explains the missing provider risk in template creation, and recommends using React Flow useNodes instead.

output_examples.input: Review a component that passes a new object literal to a heavy child component on every render.
output_examples.output: The review identifies a performance suggestion and recommends wrapping the complex prop value in useMemo with the correct dependencies.

best_practices: Ask for a pending-change review before opening a pull request.
Provide exact file paths when you need a focused review.
Use the suggested fixes as guidance, then run the project tests or build checks.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as a complete security review for frontend code.
Do not expect it to validate behavior without repository context.
Do not apply suggestions blindly when the local design system has newer rules.

faq.question: What files does this skill review?
faq.answer: It focuses on frontend TypeScript and JavaScript files, especially React components and workflow node files.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides review instructions and checklists. It does not include executable scripts.

faq.question: Can it review pending changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports pending-change reviews and focused reviews for files named by the user.

faq.question: What makes the checklist project-specific?
faq.answer: It includes Dify rules for workflow node components, React Flow hooks, Tailwind CSS, and shared class name utilities.

faq.question: Does it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It helps surface checklist violations, but a reviewer should still verify behavior and product context.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found only documentation false positives and no executable dangerous behavior.
